chapter_summary: Chapter II explores the cognitive mechanisms behind reading, moving away from a linear model of decoding toward a model where the reader's mind actively supplies unwritten information. This process is governed by the limitations of short-term memory and the necessity of 'chunking' information using background knowledge to construct stable meanings.

claims.text: Scientific research from 1965 to 1985 has produced an unparalleled understanding of the underlying processes in language comprehension.
claims.source_passage: the past two decades of scientific research 'have produced an array of information which is unparalleled in its understanding of the underlying processes in the comprehension of language.'

claims.text: Reading does not follow an orderly, linear pattern of identifying words, then meanings, then sentences, and finally text meanings.
claims.source_passage: new research has shown that reading doesn't follow an orderly pattern, as used to be thought. We don't first identify words, then word meanings, next combine word meanings to get the meanings of sentences, and finally combine sentence meanings to get the meaning of a whole text.

claims.text: The linear, bottom-up model of reading is oversimplified and presents a highly misleading picture of how we understand texts.
claims.source_passage: This model isn't wrong in all respects, but we know that it is so oversimplified and incomplete that it presents a highly misleading picture of the way we understand texts.

claims.text: The reader's mind is not just a decoder of written text but an active supplier of essential information that is not written down.
claims.source_passage: It brings to the fore the highly active mind of the reader, who is now discovered to be not only a decoder of what is written down but also a supplier of much essential information that is not written down.

claims.text: The explicit meanings of a text are only the 'tip of the iceberg'; the majority of meaning resides in the reader's relevant background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The explicit meanings of a piece of writing are the tip of an iceberg of meaning; the larger part lies below the surface of the text and is composed of the reader's own relevant knowledge.

claims.text: Human short-term memory cannot reliably hold more than about four to seven separate items.
claims.source_passage: The mind cannot reliably hold in short-term memory more than about four to seven separate items, whether numbers, letters, points on the body, degrees of loudness, degrees of warmth, and so on.

claims.text: The limitation of short-term memory is a universal constraint that applies to every domain of experience, not just language.
claims.source_passage: Miller's observation holds true for discrete items in every domain of experience, not just those presented in language.

claims.text: The capacity limit of short-term memory is hard-wired into the human system and cannot be directly overcome, even by memory experts.
claims.source_passage: Memory experts have worked out indirect tricks to circumvent this universal limitation, but they can never directly overcome it, because the constraint is hard-wired into our systems.

claims.text: Failure to perform tasks reliably occurs whenever a task forces the mind to exceed the capacity of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Whenever we try to perform a task that consistently forces us to exceed the capacity of short-term memory, we fail to perform it reliably.

claims.text: The cognitive technique of 'chunking' allows the mind to circumvent the built-in limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is 'chunking.' We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

claims.text: Chunking words into phrasal units allows a reader to process sentences that exceed the literal item-limit of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: I am able to chunk words into phrasal units and apply my long-term memory capacities as well. How do I manage this?

claims.text: Short-term memory serves as a momentary suspension area that allows the mind to convert incoming items into a stable structure.
claims.source_passage: Short-term memory holds in momentary suspension items that have come in one after another, thus enabling us to convert them into a stable structure.

claims.text: Short-term memory allows the mind to convert the temporal flow of language into non-temporal structures.
claims.source_passage: Amidst the temporal flow of language, short-term memory allows us to form a few words into nontemporal structures.

claims.text: Every known language divides sentences into clausal units because of the capacity limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: The limitation imposed by short-term memory accounts for the fact that all languages must form brief bursts of words into clauses. Every known language divides its sentences into semicomplete clausal units that are small enough to be structured within the limitations of short-term memory.

claims.text: The clause is the primary perceptual unit of language processing.
claims.source_passage: As the psycholinguist T. G. Bever explains: "(1) The clause is the primary perceptual unit; (2) within each clause we assign semantic relations within major phrases; (3) after each clause is processed, it is recoded into relatively abstract form, thereby leaving short-term memory available for processing the next clause."

claims.text: Language is transferred from short-term memory to long-term memory as a shorthand recoding of the 'gist' rather than a literal recollection of words.
claims.source_passage: Bever says that language is transferred from short-term memory into long-term memory not as a literal recollection of words but as a shorthand recoding of their gist, which normally erases from memory many of the individual words.

claims.text: It is evolutionarily more desirable to prioritize the memory of meanings over the absolute memory of surface details/literal words.
claims.source_passage: From an evolutionary standpoint it is much more desirable that we forget the literal words we encounter and remember their meanings than that we have an absolute memory for both words and meanings.

claims.text: Surface forms of sentences are lost to memory within a few seconds.
claims.source_passage: In fact, experimenters have found that surface forms of sentences are lost to memory within a few seconds.

claims.text: The original surface form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.
claims.source_passage: Sachs inferred from these results, and later experimentation has amply confirmed her inference, that the original form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.

claims.text: Subjects do not notice when a synonym is substituted in a test sentence if the substitution preserves the original meaning.
claims.source_passage: In 1966 S. Fillenbaum showed that subjects don't notice that a synonym has been substituted in a test sentence.

claims.text: Linguistic inferences regarding synonyms and implications are made at the time a sentence is understood, not later during recognition or recall.
claims.source_passage: Most remarkable of all, it was discovered about a decade later that inferences regarding open versus not closed and short versus not tall are made at the time a sentence is understood, not later in recognition or recall.

claims.text: Accurate memory of the surface form of language normally disappears in a few seconds.
claims.source_passage: it is known that accurate memory of surface form normally disappears in a few seconds, while accurate memory for gist is quite persistent.

claims.text: Listeners lose the exact wording of a sentence after as few as sixteen intervening syllables.
claims.source_passage: Eric Wanner showed that listeners had already lost the exact wording of a sentence when only sixteen syllables intervened between the sentence and the test.

claims.text: Initial understanding of a text depends on the application of relevant background knowledge that is not provided in the text itself.
claims.source_passage: In a series of experiments they found that our initial understanding of a text depends on our applying relevant background knowledge that is not given in the text itself.

claims.text: Human language comprehension involves constructing an elaborated model of what the words imply and storing that model rather than the literal literal meanings of clauses.
claims.source_passage: According to the other theory, we construct an elaborated model of what the words imply and store that. Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text's literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text.

claims.text: People store the fuller model of a text's meaning in long-term memory rather than just the explicit words.
claims.source_passage: We then store the fuller model of the text's meaning in long-term memory.

claims.text: Subjects consistently mistake implied information for explicit wording if the implication aligns with their background knowledge and mental model of the situation.
claims.source_passage: Subjects consistently made this mistake about the explicit words of the sentence. The constructive hypothesis, in other [words, was supported].

claims.text: Readers consistently go beyond a text's literal meanings to supply important implications not explicitly stated by the words.
claims.source_passage: Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text's literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text.

claims.text: The constructive hypothesis of verbal meaning has been reconfirmed many times and is widely accepted in psychology.
claims.source_passage: The constructive hypothesis, in other words, proved to be correct... This constructive hypothesis of verbal meaning has been closely studied and reconfirmed many times in the past several years.

claims.text: Humans possess poor memory for specific verbal phrasing but high memory for the underlying meaning of sentences.
claims.source_passage: The result was not altogether surprising in light of Sachs's demonstration that we have bad memory for words but good memory for meaning.

claims.text: Background knowledge is an intrinsic part of the meaning of a text from the moment of understanding.
claims.source_passage: The answer given by this and other experiments is 'Yes, that is the nature of verbal meaning: inferences based on prior knowledge are part of meaning from the very beginning.'

claims.text: There is a recursive, mutual influence between a reader's mental models and the specific words of a text during the reading process.
claims.source_passage: From this finding we can predict that there will be mutual influences between the mental models we have in our minds on the basis of prior knowledge and the words of the text as we read them.

claims.text: Readers must use relevant prior knowledge to construct a mental model in order for sentence meanings to hang together logically.
claims.source_passage: To make sense of what we read, we must use relevant prior knowledge to form a model of how sentence meanings hang together.

claims.text: Text that is linguistically simple but informationally vague cannot be understood or remembered without a specific context that invokes prior knowledge.
claims.source_passage: They used a passage written in language so general and vague that, in the absence of a context, it was difficult to construct a mental model from it. But if the passage was given a title that invoked relevant prior knowledge, subjects constructed a mental model that enabled them to understand and remember the sentences.

claims.text: An integrated mental model is essential for the basic understanding and retention of discourse.
claims.source_passage: Apparently such an integrated model is essential to understanding and remembering discourse.

claims.text: Individuals lacking cultural knowledge are functionally in the same position as experimental subjects trying to read a vague text without a provided context.
claims.source_passage: Persons who lack cultural knowledge are in just the position of the subjects who were given the clothes-washing passage without benefit of a title to form a context for the sentences.

claims.text: Informationally deprived people can decode individual sentences but cannot comprehend the whole text because they miss referential clues.
claims.source_passage: Informationally deprived people constantly run across passages that look like the Bransford one, because the texts contain important referential clues they can't understand. Although they can read the individual sentences, they can't make sense out of the whole.

claims.text: Persons who lack cultural knowledge are in the same cognitive position as subjects reading the Bransford 'clothes-washing' passage without a title.
claims.source_passage: Persons  who  lack  cultural  knowledge  are  in  just the  position  of  the  subjects  who  were  given  the  clothes-washing passage  without  benefit  of  a  title  to  form  a  context  for  the  sentences.

claims.text: Informationally deprived people frequently encounter texts they cannot understand because the texts contain referential clues they lack the knowledge to decode.
claims.source_passage: Informationally  deprived  people  constantly  run  across  passages  that  look  like  the  Bransford  one,  because  the  texts  contain  important referential  clues  they  can't  understand.

claims.text: It is possible to read individual sentences perfectly while remaining unable to make sense of a text as a whole due to a lack of background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Although  they  can  read  the individual  sentences,  they  can't  make  sense  out  of  the  whole.

claims.text: The difficulty community college students had with the Grant and Lee passage in 1978 was caused by ignorance of the historical identities of the figures, not a lack of vocabulary skills.
claims.source_passage: The  community  college  test  population  found  this  passage  difficult to  understand  because  they  were,  surprisingly,  ignorant  of  the identities  of  Grant  and  Lee.  They  were  not  stumped  by  the  vocabulary  of  the  text  in  the  ordinary  sense  of  that  term.

claims.text: Variations in reading skill can be accurately measured by observing variations in the relevant background knowledge of the audience.
claims.source_passage: We had unwittingly discovered a way to measure the variations in reading skill attributable to variations in the relevant background knowledge of audiences.

claims.text: Readers show a definite sensitivity to the stylistic superiority of a text only when the topic is familiar to them.
claims.source_passage: As  long  as  both  versions  of  a  text  concerned  a  subject  familiar to  the  readers,  their  performances  showed  a  definite  sensitivity  to the  stylistic  superiority  of  one  version  over  the  other.

claims.text: When a text's topic is unfamiliar, the stylistic quality of the writing (well-written vs. stylistically impaired) has no significant effect on reading performance.
claims.source_passage: When  the topic  was  familiar,  the  group  reading  the  better  version  understood it  more  efficiently;  when  it  was  unfamiliar,  the  performances  of the  two  groups  was  nearly  the  same.

claims.text: Reading rate is an informative variable for measuring reading efficiency when comprehension levels are held constant.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension  tests  determined  that  all  subjects  had  understood the  passages,  making  reading  rate  an  informative  variable.

claims.text: Reading rate is an informative variable for measuring comprehension performance provided that subjects have understood the passages.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension tests determined that all subjects had understood the passages, making reading rate an informative variable.

claims.text: When reading familiar topics, audiences perform better on stylistically superior texts than on stylistically degraded versions.
claims.source_passage: The dotted line represents the performance of the group that was given the superior text, the solid line the performance of the group given the stylistically degraded version.

claims.text: A lack of familiarity with a topic debases the reading rates of audiences.
claims.source_passage: Lack of familiarity not only debased the reading rates of our audiences, it also erased the differential effects of good and bad style.

claims.text: Lack of background knowledge erases the performance benefits normally provided by clear and good prose style.
claims.source_passage: Lack of familiarity not only debased the reading rates of our audiences, it also erased the differential effects of good and bad style.

claims.text: The primary deficiency in the reading performance of community college students is a lack of specific background information rather than a lack of reading skill.
claims.source_passage: but promising because the results with "Friendship" show that background information was really all they were lacking.

claims.text: The reading skills—including memory capacity, eye movements, and basic vocabulary—of community college students were sufficient, despite their failure to comprehend specific texts.
claims.source_passage: Their memory capacities, eye movements, basic vocabularies, and reading strategies — in short, their reading skills — were all up to par.

claims.text: Writers take for granted that their readers possess a specific body of background information, such as knowledge of the Civil War.
claims.source_passage: What they were missing was background information about Grant, Lee, and the Civil War (and, it can be inferred, much else that writers take for granted in their readers).

claims.text: Background knowledge is a critical factor in the general reading ability of children.
claims.source_passage: In experiments with children, researchers have drawn significant conclusions about the importance of background knowledge for general reading ability.

claims.text: Community college students who struggled with the 'Grant and Lee' text lacked the background information needed for general literacy.
claims.source_passage: the community college students lacked the background information needed for general literacy

claims.text: Differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children's possession of more knowledge.
claims.source_passage: differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children's possessing more knowledge

claims.text: Age-based differences in reading ability are not caused by differences in memory capacity, reasoning ability, or eye movement control.
claims.source_passage: not by differences in their memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or control of eye movements.

claims.text: Among children with identical reading and IQ scores, those with greater relevant background knowledge show superior reading performance on specific texts.
claims.source_passage: among seven-year-olds who score the same on reading and IQ tests, those who have greater knowledge relevant to the text at hand show superior reading skills.

claims.text: Educators have failed to acknowledge or act on the educational implications of background knowledge's role in comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Nevertheless, educators have not acknowledged or acted on the implications of such results.

claims.text: All human experience is ongoing, partial, and fragmented.
claims.source_passage: All continuing experience is partial and fragmented like my view of those trees.

claims.text: Readers operate within a narrow window of attention that is constrained by the limits of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: According to the picture sketched by recent research, the reader is confined to a rather narrow window of attention that is limited by short-term memory.

claims.text: Human meaning-making requires placing transitory fragments of perception within larger, non-visible wholes based on past knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In our minds these transitory fragments can acquire meaning only by being placed within larger, not presently visible wholes that are based on past knowledge.

claims.text: Humans tend to interpret experiences through middle-level categories rather than highly specific or highly general ones.
claims.source_passage: people tend to interpret their experiences through middle categories, that is, through classifications that are neither specific, like oak leaves, nor general, like flora.

claims.text: The mind initially uses categories that have proven most useful in the past as efficient instruments for acting in the world.
claims.source_passage: We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past. We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: Children learn middle-level categories first in acquiring language, learning terms like 'tree' before 'oak' and 'dog' before 'animal.'
claims.source_passage: Research has shown that middle-level categories are the ones children learn first in acquiring language; they learn tree before oak, and they learn dog before animal.

claims.text: Middle-level categories, or basic-level terms, constitute the 'basic furniture' of the human conceptual world.
claims.source_passage: middle-level categories are the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

claims.text: People understand normal classifications through commonly encountered cultural examples.
claims.source_passage: Furthermore, people understand these normal classifications through commonly encountered examples. For instance, a typical association of the category tree for North Americans is leaves, rather than fronds.

claims.text: Middle-level categories are the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.
claims.source_passage: We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: In the absence of special context or constraints, people understand the world through basic-level classifications.
claims.source_passage: She has demonstrated that, in the absence of any special context or constraint, people understand the world in terms of basic classifications.

claims.text: Members of a specific culture consistently agree on which examples are most typical of a category.
claims.source_passage: The results were quite consistent from person to person. For instance, under fruit, the subjects agreed on the following order of typicality: apple, fig, plum, cherry, watermelon, prune, coconut, olive.

claims.text: Sentences using a general class name only remain sensible when a typical member of that class is substituted.
claims.source_passage: In isolated sentences, when an untypical member of a class is substituted for the class name, sentences that previously sounded reasonable often fall into absurdity.

claims.text: Humans hold typical exemplars or prototypes of categories in their minds as their primary classifications for everyday use.
claims.source_passage: This finding suggests that we hold in our minds typical exemplars or prototypes of the category words we use and that these prototypes constitute the usual furniture of our minds.

claims.text: Category prototypes are processed much faster than atypical examples when verifying statements.
claims.source_passage: Rosch and others have shown that such prototypes are processed much faster than atypical examples in verifying the truth or falsity of isolated sentences.

claims.text: Humans possess mental prototypes that contain definite features used to verify sentences through feature-matching.
claims.source_passage: Reseachers have concluded that our usual prototypes for terms like bird, tree, and so on, contain definite features that we match up when we verify sentences like 'A chicken is a bird.'

claims.text: Understanding prototypes is essential for explaining how past knowledge is applied to the comprehension of speech.
claims.source_passage: Knowing about prototypes is essential for understanding how we apply past knowledge to the comprehension of speech.

claims.text: Current experiences are rendered meaningful by assimilating them to prototypes formed from past experiences.
claims.source_passage: We are able to make our present experiences take on meaning by assimilating them to prototypes formed from our past experiences.

claims.text: The term 'schema' (or 'schemata') refers to abstract mental entities that structure reading and comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Researchers who have been relating these mental entities to reading, particularly R. C. Anderson and his associates, have chosen the word schema for them, and it is the term I use to refer to the phenomena.

claims.text: Mental models of prototypes are identical to Hilary Putnam's concept of 'stereotypes'.
claims.source_passage: These mental models are the same as Hilary Putnam's "stereotypes."

claims.text: Schemata are abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.
claims.source_passage: Schema and its plural schemata correctly suggest somewhat abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.

claims.text: The content of a specific schema, such as that for a 'bird', is culturally dependent and varies by geographical region.
claims.source_passage: If Rosch had tested her subjects in Australia, her results would probably have been different, and she would have found the typical bird schema to be unlike a robin in many respects.

claims.text: Schemata used to interpret isolated words or sentences are not necessarily the same as the initial schemata applied in varied contexts.
claims.source_passage: They have found that the schemata under which we create and interpret isolated sentences are not necessarily the initial schemata we apply in other contexts.

claims.text: Initial schemata are highly sensitive to context.
claims.source_passage: It is clear that Rosch's work must be further generalized so that it reflects the context sensitivity of our initial schemata.

claims.text: The human mind possesses a remarkable ability to adjust initial schemata unconsciously based on situational factors.
claims.source_passage: The research with the word list just referred to has shown that our minds have a remarkable ability to change initial schemata according to the situations in which we find ourselves. Such adjustments are largely unconscious.

claims.text: In the process of reading, readers adjust their initial schemata to fit the specific details of the text.
claims.source_passage: In reading, for example, we adjust our initial schemata to the specific text. In a story about a Thanksgiving feast, our initial schema for bird is turkey, not robin.

claims.text: A 'two-way traffic' exists between a reader's schemata and the words on the page.
claims.source_passage: Two-way traffic takes place between our schemata and the words we read. We apply past schemata to make sense of the incoming words, but these words and other contextual clues affect our initial choices of schemata and our continuing adjustment of them.

claims.text: Reading is an active process of selecting and adjusting the most appropriate schemata rather than a passive reception of meaning.
claims.source_passage: Thus, the reader is not just passively receiving meaning but is actively selecting the most appropriate schemata for making sense of the incoming words. Then the reader actively adjusts those schemata to the incoming words until a good fit is achieved.

claims.text: The process of matching word meanings to schemata can work efficiently only if the reader has quick access to appropriate schemata.
claims.source_passage: This process can work efficiently only if the reader has quick access to appropriate schemata.

claims.text: When required schemata are not quickly available, the cognitive load exceeds the limits of short-term memory, causing reading comprehension to break down.
claims.source_passage: When the appropriate schemata are not quickly available, and the reader is forced to do a lot of pondering to construct them at the time of reading, the limits of short-term memory are quickly reached, and the process has to be painfully restarted and restarted.

claims.text: Cultural literacy provides the high-level schemata (like historical knowledge) necessary for efficient reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The immediately needed schemata go far beyond birds and cups to include the wider knowledge of cultural literacy.

claims.text: When appropriate schemata are not quickly available, the reader is forced to spend cognitive effort constructing them at the time of reading.
claims.source_passage: When the appropriate schemata are not quickly available, and the reader is forced to do a lot of pondering to construct them at the time of reading, the limits of short-term memory are quickly reached

claims.text: The cognitive limits of short-term memory necessitate that the reading process be repeatedly restarted if background schemata are missing.
claims.source_passage: the limits of short-term memory are quickly reached, and the process has to be painfully restarted and restarted.

claims.text: The schemata required for reading comprehension extend beyond simple object recognition to the wider knowledge base of cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: The immediately needed schemata go far beyond birds and cups to include the wider knowledge of cultural literacy.

claims.text: Readers cannot make sense of specific text passages if they do not know how the words connect to larger schemata.
claims.source_passage: If I do not know how they connect with larger schemata, I cannot make sense of them.

claims.text: The schemata needed for basic reading comprehension are abstract entities that do not require profound or detailed knowledge.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, the schemata I need are rather abstract entities that do not require profound knowledge.

claims.text: Background knowledge often exists in the mind as a schematic model rather than a literal list of propositions.
claims.source_passage: These ideas do not take the form of such a list in our minds but probably exist as a schematic model.

claims.text: Specialized or specific information is typically provided explicitly by a text, whereas the broad schemata required to frame it must be brought by the reader.
claims.source_passage: More specialized information is provided explicitly by the text.

claims.text: Short-term memory limits humans to attending to only a few salient features of word meanings at a time.
claims.source_passage: We know that, in reading, our short-term memories allow us to attend only to a few salient features of word meanings — just those that are needed to make the meanings fit together.

claims.text: Comprehension consists of the ability to supply the implied background relationships that connect surface-level meanings.
claims.source_passage: When we really comprehend what we are reading, we are able to supply those implied background relationships.

claims.text: The use of surface elements to stand for larger schematic wholes is an essential feature of human mental life caused by narrow attention windows.
claims.source_passage: Because our narrow windows of attention confine us to just a few elements at a time, the technique of using surface elements to stand for larger wholes is an essential feature of our mental life.

claims.text: Human memory has a natural tendency to 'schematize' details over time, making them more typical and average.
claims.source_passage: we have a tendency to "schematize" the details of what we remember about pictures and events so that, with a lapse of time, the details we remember become more and more like the average or typical things of our experience.

claims.text: Memories are actively constructed from habitual schemata rather than being mere reproductions of memory traces.
claims.source_passage: our memories are freshly constructed from our habitual schemata, not just reproduced from actual memory traces.

claims.text: The constructive nature of memory leads individuals to introduce elements that were not present in the original event if those elements exist in their normal schemata.
claims.source_passage: Our memories are always introducing elements from our normal schemata that weren't in an original event

claims.text: Human memory suppresses elements of original events that do not align with existing schemata.
claims.source_passage: they are always suppressing some elements of the original event that don't exist in our normal schemata.

claims.text: Individuals tend to form a general impression of a whole situation and then construct the probable details based on that impression.
claims.source_passage: In all ordinary instances he has an overmastering tendency simply to get a general impression of the whole; and on the basis of this, he constructs the probable detail.

claims.text: Very little of a person's recollection of a situation is based on literal observation.
claims.source_passage: Very little of his construction is literally observed. But it is the sort of construction which serves to justify his general impression.

claims.text: Schemata are essential instruments of human cognitive life.
claims.source_passage: All this is evidence that schemata are essential instruments of our cognitive lives.

claims.text: The tendency to associate words that are schematically connected is greater than the frequency of their past co-occurrence would suggest.
claims.source_passage: If schemata do indeed govern our associations, then our tendency to associate words that are schematically connected ought to be greater than would be expected merely from the frequency of their co-occurrence in our past experience

claims.text: Schemata function powerfully in both language comprehension and memory.
claims.source_passage: This is strong evidence that schemata function powerfully in both comprehension and memory.

claims.text: There are at least two distinct types of schemata: those resembling static pictures and those resembling scripts or procedures.
claims.source_passage: The latest view (that of Johnson-Laird, for instance) is that we use at least two radically different types of schemata, one analogous to static pictures and another to scripts or procedures.

claims.text: Human cognition utilizes both general category schemata and specific memory traces of individual perceptions.
claims.source_passage: In addition, we have not only general schemata of categories like birds but also memory traces of occasions when we perceived or read about birds.

claims.text: Schemata are provisional theories that are open to revision.
claims.source_passage: we treat them as provisional theories open to revision, and so forth.

claims.text: Educational practice does not require an exhaustive understanding of the fine details of cognitive modeling to improve literacy instruction.
claims.source_passage: But we do not have to know the fine detail of the process to make intelligent practical advances in the teaching of literacy.

claims.text: Schemata serve two essential functions for literacy: storing knowledge in retrievable form and organizing it for efficient deployment.
claims.source_passage: We know that schemata perform two essential functions that are relevant to literacy. The first is storing knowledge in retrievable form; the second is organizing knowledge in more and more efficient ways, so that it can be applied rapidly and efficiently.

claims.text: Appropriate background knowledge is a prerequisite for understanding written or spoken language.
claims.source_passage: Without appropriate background knowledge, people cannot adequately understand written or spoken language.

claims.text: No good reader is a very slow reader.
claims.source_passage: Although there are sizable variations in reading rates among good readers, no good reader is a very slow reader.

claims.text: Reading slowness beyond a certain threshold prevents the assimilation of complex meaning.
claims.source_passage: Slowness of reading beyond a certain point makes assimilation of complex meaning impossible.

claims.text: The limits of short-term memory prevent the integration of information that has not been 'chunked' by schemata.
claims.source_passage: The limits of short-term memory do not allow the integration of "unchunked" material, and so crucial parts of meaning are lost to memory while other parts are being painstakingly worked out.

claims.text: Schemas function as a shorthand that allows a single surface element to represent complex subterranean elements, helping to bypass the limits of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: One element of a schema represents many other subterranean elements, allowing us to deploy that one surface element as though the whole complex schema were a single item.

claims.text: In the process of comprehension, speed of information manipulation is equivalent to quality of comprehension.
claims.source_passage: In this process, speed of comprehension is equivalent to quality of comprehension, because without the speed and the shorthand provided by well-organized schemata our circuits get overloaded.

claims.text: Information that is most frequently needed is stored at the 'surface' of a schema and is most directly available for retrieval.
claims.source_passage: Their theory is that the knowledge most often needed is also the most directly available and is, so to speak, right at the surface of the schema.

claims.text: Determining the truth of a statement takes longer the 'deeper' into a schematic network a person must go to retrieve the relevant information.
claims.source_passage: The prediction is that, if knowledge is organized in this kind of pattern, the decision whether sentences are true or false will take longer the farther down the vertical path the sentences require us to go.

claims.text: Secondary or remote factual information about an object is not important for effective human communication.
claims.source_passage: But this secondary information about canaries is not important in communicating with fellow human beings.

claims.text: Literacy and conversation depend on the rapid deployment of primary cultural associations distinctive to a specific term.
claims.source_passage: What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata we use — the traits that differentiate canaries from other birds: their smallness, yellowness, ability to sing, use in human culture, being kept in cages, and so on.

claims.text: Primary associations must be called up in milliseconds; there is no substitute for simply knowing them in advance.
claims.source_passage: There is no substitute for simply knowing these primary associations. They must be called up with lightning speed in the course of reading and conversing.

claims.text: Successful communication is fundamentally dependent on shared associations.
claims.source_passage: Successful communication depends upon shared associations.

claims.text: Participation in a literate national culture requires acquiring the specific information that is shared within that culture.
claims.source_passage: To participate in the literate national culture is to have acquired a sense of the information that is shared in that culture.

claims.text: Ordinary discourse assumes a baseline of 'rudiments of knowledge' to avoid becoming too intricate and lengthy to understand.
claims.source_passage: No adult-level discourse retreats to the rudiments of knowledge. If assumptions about rudiments could not be made, ordinary discourse would be so lengthy and intricate as to obscure its own point.

claims.text: Educators in the Rousseau-Dewey tradition emphasize the intensive study of a few cases over the acquisition of facts.
claims.source_passage: Educators in the Rousseau-Dewey tradition, who favor less emphasis on mere fact and more emphasis on the intensive study of a few cases,

claims.text: Adult-level discourse does not explain the rudiments of knowledge because doing so would make discourse too lengthy and intricate to be clear.
claims.source_passage: No adult-level discourse retreats to the rudiments of knowledge. If assumptions about rudiments could not be made, ordinary discourse would be so lengthy and intricate as to obscure its own point.

claims.text: Literacy requires both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.
claims.source_passage: But literacy requires us to have both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.

claims.text: Dictionaries and encyclopedias are impractical tools for comprehension if a reader lacks crucial, quickly available background information.
claims.source_passage: When readers constantly lack crucial information, dictionaries and encyclopedias become quite impractical tools.

claims.text: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious that it fails to convey meaning.
claims.source_passage: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious and uncommunicative that it fails to convey meaning.

claims.text: All cognitive skills are knowledge-bound.
claims.source_passage: Researchers in cognitive psychology and the area of computer science known as artificial intelligence (AI) have come to strikingly similar conclusions about the knowledge-bound character of all cognitive skills.

claims.text: It is more accurate to speak of 'reading skills' (plural) than 'reading skill' (singular).
claims.source_passage: It is more accurate to speak of "reading skills" than of "reading skill."

claims.text: Cognitive skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed through general calisthenics.
claims.source_passage: a skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.

claims.text: There are no general, transferable skills in problem solving or critical thinking.
claims.source_passage: Researchers have consistently found that people do not develop general, transferable skills in problem solving, critical thinking, or in any other field.

claims.text: Once relevant knowledge has been acquired, the exercise of a skill follows automatically.
claims.source_passage: Once the relevant knowledge has been acquired, the skill follows.

claims.text: Experts perform better than novices because they have more relevant and quickly available information, not more powerful intellectual machinery.
claims.source_passage: AI research shows that experts perform better than novices not because they have more powerful and better oiled intellectual machinery but because they have more relevant and quickly available information.

claims.text: Good readers are distinguished from poor ones simply by the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.
claims.source_passage: What distinguishes good readers from poor ones is simply the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.

claims.text: Expertise in a field does not imply superior general memory or general problem-solving skills.
claims.source_passage: In his second experiment de Groot proved that expert chess players have no better memories or general skills than novices.

claims.text: Human skill is domain-specific and does not transfer to new problems when the configuration of a task is significantly changed.
claims.source_passage: When the configuration of a task is significantly changed, past skills are not transferred to the new problem.

claims.text: Expert performance depends on the quick deployment of stored schemata.
claims.source_passage: Both approaches show that expert performance depends on the quick deployment of schemata.

claims.text: Human short-term memory is limited to holding approximately five or six individual items.
claims.source_passage: When de Groot's subjects were exposed to a chessboard for about six seconds, the limits of short-term memory were quickly reached. . . the range predicted by research [is] only about six pieces.

claims.text: Experts perceive information in 'chunks' of five or six pieces per schema, which alleviates the burden on short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Think of the experts as perceiving the positions of five or six pieces all at once, as words rather than individual letters. Because they already possess positional schemata, experts would need to remember only four or five chunked items — well within the range of short-term memory.

claims.text: Possessing specific schemata facilitates cognitive tasks by turning multiple items into a single 'chunk' of information.
claims.source_passage: Because they already possess positional schemata, experts would need to remember only four or five chunked items — well within the range of short-term memory.

claims.text: The convergence of artificial intelligence models and empirical psychology increases the reliability of knowledge-based models of human skill.
claims.source_passage: The convergence of the accounts from both AI and empirical psychology increases one's confidence in their correctness.

claims.text: Effective artificial intelligence programs succeed by imitating the knowledge-based procedures of human experts rather than using brute-force calculation.
claims.source_passage: An up-to-date chess program behaves like a skilled player. . . because in some ways, though not in all, it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

claims.text: The results found in chess expertise research are applicable to other academic fields like algebra, physics, and medicine.
claims.source_passage: This experiment has been duplicated in several different laboratories, and structurally in several other fields, including algebra, physics, and medicine, always with the same striking results.

claims.text: Expert performance in chess and reading relies on the recall of patterns rather than the processing of individual data points.
claims.source_passage: When de Groot's chess masters were asked to reproduce the original position, they needed only to recall two or three variations and were able to reconstruct the rest from the schemata they already possessed.

claims.text: Reading skill and chess skill are analogous in their requirement for the rapid deployment of pre-acquired schemata.
claims.source_passage: Skill in reading is like skill in chess in many respects. Good reading, like good chess, requires the rapid deployment of schemata that have already been acquired and do not have to be worked out on the spot.

claims.text: Good readers ignore small-scale features of a text to free up short-term memory for overall structural meaning.
claims.source_passage: Good readers, like good chess players, quickly recognize typical patterns, and, since they can ignore many small-scale features of the text, they have space in short-term memory to take in an overall structure of meaning.

claims.text: Unskilled readers experience memory overload because they must process small-scale meaning relationships due to a lack of relevant schemata.
claims.source_passage: By contrast, unskilled readers lack this large store of relevant schemata and must therefore work out many small-scale meaning relationships while they are reading. These demanding tasks quickly overload their short-term memories, making their performance slow, arduous, and ineffective.

claims.text: There is an upper limit of approximately 50,000 rapidly accessible schemata for any domain of human activity.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, a number in the range of 50,000 marks the upper limit of items to which we can have rapid access in any domain of activity. Using a significantly greater number of items would cause the process of search and retrieval to occupy too much time.

claims.text: A basic vocabulary of 50,000 words or idioms serves as a functional index for a much larger volume of knowledge in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: A basic vocabulary of 50,000 schemata serves merely as a quickly accessible index to a much larger volume of knowledge.

claims.text: Effective mental organization requires keeping indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.
claims.source_passage: As we have seen, the most effective system for the mind, whether in chess or in reading, is to keep most of its indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.

claims.text: Unlike chess schemata, verbal schemata must be shared across the language community to enable communication.
claims.source_passage: The patterns of association in the verbal schemata of one person must approximate those of another, otherwise we could not use surface meanings to represent larger systems of subsurface associations.

claims.text: Words and idioms represent systems of association that belong to the language community as a whole, not just the individual.
claims.source_passage: Words and idioms therefore represent systems of association that belong not just to the individual mind but to the language community as a whole.

claims.text: The systems of subsurface associations held by one person must approximate those of another for surface meanings to function.
claims.source_passage: The associations of one person must approximate those of another, otherwise we could not use surface meanings to represent larger systems of subsurface associations.

claims.text: Cultural literacy consists of the shared systems of association represented by words, idioms, and grammatical systems.
claims.source_passage: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems represent shared systems of association — cultural literacy.

claims.text: A primary constraint of reading is the lack of conversational access, gestures, or facial expressions between author and reader.
claims.source_passage: One constraint of reading is that author and reader cannot converse with each other. Only the author is the source of the words; the reader has no access to an author's gestures or facial expressions.

claims.text: Literate adults perform communication tasks involving unfamiliar shapes with virtually no errors on the first attempt.
claims.source_passage: To establish a standard of performance, we run the experiment with pairs of literate adults, who find the task almost ridiculously easy. They make virtually no errors on their first attempt.

claims.text: Young children (ages four to five) produce short and cryptic messages in communication tasks because they rely on unshared associations.
claims.source_passage: Their messages are short and cryptic rather than full and elaborated like those of adults. Moreover, the messages depend upon associations that are not widely shared in the speech community.

claims.text: Kindergartners show no improvement with practice in complex communication tasks, similar to nursery school children.
claims.source_passage: Kindergartners performed no better than nursery school children, and displayed the same lack of improvement with practice.

claims.text: Even ninth-graders fail to reach adult levels of accuracy on the first trial of complex communication tasks.
claims.source_passage: Ninth-graders, although they showed dramatic improvement in successive trials, still did not attain the virtually perfect accuracy that adults displayed from the very first trial.

claims.text: The poor performance of older children on communication tasks is caused by a lack of information rather than insufficient cognitive development.
claims.source_passage: Krauss and Glucksberg inferred from this — correctly, I believe — that the older children performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development but because of their lack of information.

claims.text: Children perform poorly because they do not realize the extent to which their internal schemata are not shared with the community.
claims.source_passage: They had not acquired an easy sense of the degree to which their schemata were shared with strangers in the speech community.

claims.text: A lack of information strains cognitive capacities when individuals attempt to carry out communication tasks.
claims.source_passage: Their lack of information strained their cognitive capacities when they tried to carry out the task.

claims.text: Children engage in successful, social, nonegocentric speech when the demands of the task are relatively light.
claims.source_passage: When the demands of the task are relatively light, children do engage in social, nonegocentric speech, and they communicate rather successfully.

claims.text: Heavy task demands can prevent children from utilizing the social-communication skills they already possess.
claims.source_passage: Finally, when the demands of the task become heavy enough, children may not have the opportunity to bring into play the social-communication skills they possess.

claims.text: Children often perform poorly on communication tasks not because of insufficient cognitive development but because of their lack of information.
claims.source_passage: They performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development but because of their lack of information.

claims.text: Children lack an easy sense of the degree to which their personal schemata are shared with strangers in the wider speech community.
claims.source_passage: They had not acquired an easy sense of the degree to which their schemata were shared with strangers in the speech community.

claims.text: The performance failures of young children in communication experiments are identical in mechanism to the performance of poor readers.
claims.source_passage: The performance of the young children in this experiment is like the performance of poor readers. Both are ineffective because of cognitive overload.

claims.text: Effective reading requires the reader to monitor whether their personal schematic associations are appropriate and shared by the wider speech community.
claims.source_passage: In effective reading, one must not only call up one's own schematic associations but also monitor whether they are appropriate ones shared by the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literate adults have internalized shared schemata to the point that they function as 'second nature.'
claims.source_passage: Literate adults have internalized these shared schemata and have made them second nature.

claims.text: Semiliterates and young children lack readily accessible information regarding what information is shared by others.
claims.source_passage: Thus, besides their lack of substantive information, young children and semiliterates also lack something equally important: readily accessible information about what is shared by others.

claims.text: To communicate effectively, it is essential to know the other person's unspoken systems of association.
claims.source_passage: In reading and writing, as in communicating shapes across a physical barrier, it is essential to know the other person's unspoken systems of association.

claims.text: Cultural knowledge of shared associations is not strictly a function of chronological age.
claims.source_passage: Such cultural knowledge is not entirely a function of age. Some children acquire it rather early, and illiterate adults never acquire it fully.

claims.text: A child's performance on communication tasks correlates strongly with social class because class determines exposure to the wider speech community.
claims.source_passage: Experiments reported by Basil Bernstein suggest that the performances of children attempting tasks similar to those in the Krauss-Glucksberg experiments vary according to the children's degree of literacy, and their performances therefore have, under present circumstances, a strong correlation with social class.

claims.text: The critical variable in achieving literacy is whether children have experience communicating with literate strangers.
claims.source_passage: In Bernstein's experiments, the most important variable was whether the children were used to talking with literate strangers and had thereby gained a sense of the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literacy is fundamentally a function of a national culture rather than a local culture.
claims.source_passage: Bernstein's experiments reconfirmed that literacy is a function of a national rather than a local culture.

claims.text: The linear, bottom-up model of reading is not wrong in all respects, but its oversimplification makes it a misleading representation of language comprehension.
claims.source_passage: This model isn't wrong in all respects, but we know that it is so oversimplified and incomplete that it presents a highly misleading picture of the way we understand texts.

claims.text: Short-term memory functions as a mental vestibule that allows the mind to convert the temporal flow of language into nontemporal structures.
claims.source_passage: Short-term memory is the mind's vestibule where incoming items enjoy a brief equality lasting just long enough for the mind to give them a structure. ... short-term memory allows us to form a few words into nontemporal structures.

claims.text: The evolution of language and memory favors the loss of surface form because literal memory for every experience would exceed the mind's storage capacity.
claims.source_passage: If we remembered all the surface details of our experiences, including all the words we ever heard or read, the floppy disks in our minds would quickly fill to capacity, and we would have to erase them periodically.

claims.text: The tendency to interpret experiences through middle-level categories exists because these classifications are the most efficient instruments for acting in the world.
claims.source_passage: We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past. We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: The upper limit of approximately 50,000 rapidly accessible schemata is determined by the speed of search and retrieval processes.
claims.source_passage: There must be an upper limit to the number of schemata that we can effectively utilize, because we must have immediate access to them if they are to be effective. ... Using a significantly greater number of items would cause the process of search and retrieval to occupy too much time.

claims.text: The efficacy of artificial intelligence programs serves as proof for knowledge-based models of human skill because these programs succeed by imitating human procedures.
claims.source_passage: There is another reason why the AI research results should be taken seriously. AI models actually work. You can play chess against a computer program and lose. ... it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

claims.text: Education should focus on providing primary associations that can be retrieved in milliseconds, as readers cannot afford to figure out word associations during active comprehension.
claims.source_passage: There is no substitute for simply knowing these primary associations. They must be called up with lightning speed in the course of reading and conversing. ... we cannot pause over many words at a time.

claims.text: The circular logic that attributes expert performance to a 'skill' fails to explain the underlying mechanics of cognitive success.
claims.source_passage: Dr. Herbert A. Simon... once wryly remarked that saying an expert performance is caused by a 'skill' is like Moliere's doctor saying that the sleep-inducing properties of opium are caused by its 'dormative power.'

claims.text: In reading and writing, knowing the other person's unspoken systems of association is as essential as substantive information.
claims.source_passage: In reading and writing, as in communicating shapes across a physical barrier, it is essential to know the other person's unspoken systems of association.

evidence.description: George Miller's research on the 4-7 item limit of short-term memory across various sensory domains.
The difference in difficulty between memorizing a raw string of numbers (8-0-3-2-9-6-2-6-3-2) and a chunked string (803-296-26-32).
The ability to recall 16 individual letters when organized into a sentence ('The cat is on the mat') compared to the inability to recall 16 random letters after one presentation.
Psycholinguistic theory regarding the clause as the primary perceptual unit and its recoding into abstract form to free short-term memory.

Experiment showing that subjects quickly lose the ability to distinguish between a target sentence ('He sent a letter about it to Galileo...') and a passive-voice variation, but can distinguish it from a sentence with a different meaning.
evidence.source_passage: Sachs inferred from these results, and later experimentation has amply confirmed her inference, that the original form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.

evidence.description: Experiment showing subjects do not notice when a synonym is substituted in a recognition test ('The window is not closed').
evidence.source_passage: In 1966 S. Fillenbaum showed that subjects don't notice that a synonym has been substituted in a test sentence.

evidence.description: Sachs's recognition probe study showing subjects forget the original sentence form but remember the meaning.
Fillenbaum's experiment where subjects chose 'The window is open' as the sentence they heard when the original was 'The window is not closed'.
Wanner's study showing word-for-word memory loss after sixteen syllables of intervening text.

The Barclay, Bransford, and Franks 'turtles and log' experiment demonstrating that background knowledge of spatial relations (logs and fish) causes subjects to 'remember' implied information as explicit text.
evidence.source_reference: Barclay, J. R., Bransford, J. D., & Franks, J. J. (1972)

evidence.description: Sachs's demonstration showing that human memory quickly loses exact wording while retaining core meaning.
The 1972 Bransford experiment using a vague passage about 'Washing Clothes,' where subjects given the title before reading performed significantly better in recognition and comprehension.
Experiments using pictures instead of titles to provide the necessary model for understanding vague discourse.
Observation of community college students in 1978 struggling with a passage about Grant and Lee despite being able to read the words.

A 1978 study of community college students reading an excerpt from Bruce Catton's 'Grant and Lee'. It showed that students who understood the vocabulary still failed to comprehend the text because they did not know who Grant and Lee were.

The 'A Good Friend is Hard to Find' test passage, which used complex vocabulary (burdensome, reliability, availability) but was easily understood and recalled by students because the topic (friendship) was familiar.
A large-scale experiment involving over 700 subjects comparing reading rates for well-written vs. stylistically degraded versions of the same text across familiar and unfamiliar topics.
Quintile graphs comparing reading rates for 'Time vs. Newsweek' articles, used to demonstrate that stylistic superiority correlates with higher reading rates only when the subject matter is familiar.
Quintile graph comparison of Time vs Newsweek texts showing style improves reading rate when topics are familiar.
Experiments using texts on 'Epicurean Rome' and 'Hegel' from Durant's History of Civilization, showing that unfamiliar topics eliminate the advantage of superior style.
Comparison of University and Community College reading rates for Catton's 'Grant and Lee', showing CC students did not benefit from better style on this historical topic.
Comparison of University and Community College reading rates for an essay on 'Friendship', showing CC students performed similarly to university students when the topic was familiar.
Research by T. Trabasso on reading ability differences between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds regarding background knowledge.
The Grant and Lee / Friendship experiment results comparing community college students' comprehension based on background knowledge.
P. D. Pearson's experiment showing that second-graders with knowledge of spiders out-performed peers with equal reading scores but no spider knowledge.
The 'Tree' observation thought experiment: people identify fragmented views of branches as 'trees' rather than specific parts or general nature.
Eleanor Rosch's research on 'basic-level terms' and their primacy in human conceptualization.
Experimental ranking of category members (fruit, furniture, bird, clothing) by typicality.
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, E. (1975). Cognitive representations of semantic categories.
evidence.source_passage: In one experiment Rosch asked her subjects to arrange some specific names under the categories fruit, furniture, bird, and clothing, ranking the names from the most to the least typical.

evidence.description: Sentence substitution experiment where specific class members replace general class names, revealing limits of sense-making.
Reaction time measurements for verifying sentences like 'A robin is a bird' versus 'A chicken is a bird.'
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, E. (1973). On the internal structure of perceptual and semantic categories.
evidence.source_passage: For instance, it takes a subject much longer to say true or false to the sentence 'A chicken is a bird' than to 'A robin is a bird.'

evidence.description: Eleanor Rosch's prototype theory, specifically the identification of a robin as the 'typical' bird for North American subjects.
Variable interpretations of the words 'cup', 'eat', 'red', and 'held' based on sentence context.
evidence.source_reference: Anderson et al., cited in note 27

evidence.description: R.C. Anderson's linguistic example showing how the word 'kick' and 'ball' are interpreted differently based on the subject (punter, baby, golfer).
The 'Thanksgiving feast' context shifts the bird prototype from a robin to a turkey.
The Grant and Lee passage from a community college reading test, showing how historical surface references (Appomattox, Civil War) rely on subsurface knowledge.
Observations by Franz Wulf in 1922 regarding the tendency of memory to 'schematize' details of pictures and events into 'typical' versions.
F. C. Bartlett's research in his book 'Remembering', establishing that memory is fresh construction based on habitual schemata.
Bartlett's memory tests involving an American Indian folktale, where subjects distorted the story to fit their own cultural schemata.
Experiments using word sets with schematic associations (e.g., golf-related words) showed recall performance far superior to what stimulus-response theory predicted.
evidence.source_reference: B. H. Ross and G. H. Bower (1981)

evidence.description: Collins and Quillian's 1969 study on semantic networks showed that subjects take longer to verify sentences that require traversing more levels of a hierarchy (e.g., 'canaries can breathe' vs. 'canaries are yellow').
evidence.source_reference: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian (1969)

evidence.description: The example of U.S. Grant illustrates that for a reader to understand a text about Grant, they must instantly associate him with being a Union general, a president, and a drinker.
Community college students displayed high skill reading about friendship but low skill reading about Grant and Lee, demonstrating task-specific reading ability.
AI research demonstrating that human skill exercise depends on possessing numerous and detailed specific schemata.
Herbert Simon's work since the 1950s on the detailed structure of cognitive skills which casts doubt on general transferable skills.
Adriaan de Groot's experiments showing chess grand masters have superior memory for actual game positions (100% accuracy) compared to weaker players.
evidence.source_reference: Adriaan de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker

evidence.description: Adriaan de Groot's experiment showing Grand Masters can reproduce a 25-piece board with 100% accuracy after 5-10 seconds of exposure, while novices recall only 5-6 pieces.
evidence.source_reference: Adriaan de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker

evidence.description: De Groot's variation experiment where experts performed no better than novices when chess pieces were placed randomly on the board.
evidence.source_reference: Adriaan de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker

evidence.description: The replication of de Groot's structural findings in other professional domains including algebra, physics, and medicine.
AI chess programs that defeat human players by using knowledge-based procedures rather than checking every possible variation.
The thought experiment regarding the ease of remembering the sixteen letters in the sentence 'The cat is on the mat' due to spelling schemata.
De Groot's experiment on chess masters' ability to reconstruct positions from memory based on known patterns.
Simon and Barenfeld's estimate that a chess master recognizes approximately 50,000 positional patterns.
The statistical coincidence between the number of positional patterns a chess master knows (50,000) and the vocabulary size of a literate person (50,000).
Mathematical symbols as an example of how a single schema can compress decades of cognitive labor into a single manipulable unit.
The term 'Civil War' cited as a schema representing vast ranges of associated information stored in memory.
A laboratory experiment using a physical barrier to simulate the constraints of reading/writing while subjects describe unfamiliar shapes.
evidence.source_reference: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg, 'Social and Nonsocial Speech' (1977)
evidence.source_passage: Imagine an experiment in which some of the special constraints of reading and writing are acted out physically in the laboratory... The speaker must explain to the listener the order in which the six following unfamiliar shapes must be listed.

evidence.description: Comparison of adult shape descriptions (using shared analogies like 'motorboat' or 'spaceman') versus child descriptions (using idiosyncratic labels like 'Daddy's shirt').
evidence.source_passage: Their messages are short and cryptic rather than full and elaborated like those of adults. Moreover, the messages depend upon associations that are not widely shared in the speech community.

evidence.description: Testing across multiple grade levels (K-9) showed that even 10-year-olds and 14-year-olds do not match the immediate accuracy of adults.
evidence.source_passage: Considering that adults made virtually no errors on the very first trial, the performance of children in the first, third, and fifth grades is even more surprising: they were no better than kindergartners on the first trial.

evidence.description: Experiments involving children describing strange shapes across a physical barrier to strangers.
Experiments showing that performance on communication tasks varies by literacy degree and social class, depending on exposure to literate strangers.

concepts.author_definition: A special function of the mind that lasts just milliseconds, holding 4-7 discrete items in momentary suspension to allow the mind to structure them.
The storage of information that lasts from a few seconds to a lifetime.
A cognitive device used to resist memory limitations by grouping disparate items into a smaller number of meaningful groupings.
A reader who functions as an active participant by supplying background knowledge and inferring meanings not explicitly stated in the text.
The shorthand recoding of the meaning of a sentence or clause that is stored in long-term memory after literal words are forgotten.
The precise, literal wording and grammatical structure of a sentence, which is lost rapidly during processing.
The theory that language comprehension involves building an elaborated mental model of what a text implies, including information not explicitly stated, and storing that model in memory.
concepts.common_alternative: The theory that we interpret and store only the literal meanings of clauses and sentences.
concepts.source_passage: According to the other theory, we construct an elaborated model of what the words imply and store that. Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text's literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text.

concepts.author_definition: An integrated cognitive structure formed by combining prior knowledge with textual information that allows a reader to understand how sentences hang together.
Individuals who lack the necessary cultural background knowledge (referential clues) to construct the mental models required for literacy.
Implicit pointers within a text to outside knowledge (names, dates, events) that are essential for synthesizing the meaning of a whole passage beyond individual sentence decoding.
The quality of being 'well written' according to definite principles of prose, which increases reading efficiency only if background knowledge is already present.
A statistical visualization tool where an audience is divided into five groups based on reading rate to measure the impact of variables (like style) on performance.
Mechanical and cognitive processing abilities including memory capacity, eye movements, basic vocabulary, and reading strategies, distinct from topical knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Often used to include the overall ability to comprehend any text regardless of content.

concepts.author_definition: The level of background information and vocabulary shared by writers and readers in the public sphere, necessary to understand general-interest texts.
Classifications that are neither overly specific (oak) nor overly general (flora), which serve as the primary, most efficient way humans categorize experience.
concepts.source_passage: Eleanor Rosch has called these middle categories basic-level terms... the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

concepts.author_definition: A typical example of a category (like a robin for 'bird') that humans hold in their minds as a mental model for that category.
concepts.source_passage: This finding suggests that we hold in our minds typical exemplars or prototypes of the category words we use and that these prototypes constitute the usual furniture of our minds.

concepts.author_definition: An abstract mental entity, model, or script used to relate past knowledge to current experience and comprehension.
concepts.common_alternative: Frames, theories, concepts, scripts, or stereotypes.
concepts.source_passage: Schema and its plural schemata correctly suggest somewhat abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.

concepts.author_definition: The most 'typical' or representative example of a category used as a mental model for comprehension (e.g., a robin as a prototype for 'bird').
concepts.common_alternative: In social science, 'stereotype' often carries a negative connotation of prejudice, whereas Hilary Putnam and Hirsch use it as a neutral cognitive category.
concepts.source_passage: Knowing about prototypes is essential for understanding how we apply past knowledge to the comprehension of speech. These mental models are the same as Hilary Putnam's "stereotypes."

concepts.author_definition: The few specific word meanings that a reader can consciously attend to at one time, which serve as pointers to deeper subsurface information.
The psychological theory that memory is an active process of building a likely account of a whole from a general impression, rather than a passive retrieval of stored details.

The cognitive efficiency gained when a single surface element of a schema represents an entire complex of subterranean elements, allowing the mind to treat the whole complex as a single item in short-term memory.
The distinctive system of traits and cultural information commonly associated with a term (e.g., a canary's yellowness or use in cages) that must be retrieved instantly during communication.
The location within a cognitive network where the most frequently needed and directly available information is stored.
The basic, shared information that adult-level discourse assumes its audience already possesses and therefore does not explicitly explain.
The unstated, background information required to make sense of explicit text, such as knowing Grant was a Union general.
The principle that any cognitive skill is dependent on the possession of specific, task-relevant substantive schemata.
Intensive knowledge refers to understanding relationships/principles; extensive knowledge refers to a broad range of specific facts.
Stored patterns of domain-specific information (e.g., chess positions) in memory that allow a person to recognize and process complex configurations as single units.
The theoretical concept of transferable cognitive abilities like 'critical thinking' or 'problem solving' that are supposed to operate independently of specific domain knowledge.
The role of a basic vocabulary (approx. 50,000 items) as a rapidly accessible directory that points toward deeper associations in long-term memory.
The shared systems of association (words, idioms, grammar) that belong to a language community as a whole, allowing individuals to communicate subsurface meanings.
Nonegocentric communication that successfully conveys meaning by utilizing shared associations known to the listener.
concepts.common_alternative: Any verbal interaction between two people.
concepts.source_passage: When the demands of the task are relatively light, children do engage in social, nonegocentric speech, and they communicate rather successfully.

concepts.author_definition: A state in which a person has to figure out too many things at once, straining cognitive capacities and making performance ineffective.
Internalized systems of association and information that are known to be common among the wider culture and used to monitor the appropriateness of one's own associations during communication.
The national or broad cultural group, as opposed to a local or familiar group, characterized by shared unspoken systems of association.
A metaphor for short-term memory describing it as a temporary space where incoming linguistic items are held in equality until they can be structured.
The stable mental configurations into which the temporal flow of incoming language is converted during processing.

concepts.term: Basic furniture of our conceptual world
concepts.author_definition: Eleanor Rosch's term for middle-level categories that serve as the primary classifications humans use to understand the world.
A critique of using the word 'skill' as a circular explanation that describes a phenomenon without explaining its cause.
The cognitive processes whose time constraints limit the number of schemata a person can effectively utilize to approximately 50,000.
The specific members of a category (prototypes) that the mind keeps ready for use in perception and conversation.
